P.description: Not a single empirical study; symposium summary on comparative effectiveness research (CER) methods
P.demographics: Not reported
P.setting: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality (AHRQ) DEcIDE symposium, United States, 2009

I.description: Symposium discussion of methodological approaches to CER (study designs, analytic techniques, data sources)

C.description: No explicit comparator; symposium compares alternative methodological options for CER

O.primary_outcome: Identification and refinement of CER methodological best practices
O.measurement: Narrative synthesis of invited presentations and abstracts

parsed.causal_question: No empirical causal question; paper summarizes methodological guidance for conducting comparative effectiveness research.

P.description: Adults with type 2 diabetes and no prior kidney disease
P.demographics: Age- and sex-standardized; racial/ethnic groups: non-Hispanic white, Asian (Asian Indian, Chinese, Filipino), Hispanic, non-Hispanic black
P.setting: Outpatient health care organization, 2008–2010

variables: Race/ethnicity (non-Hispanic white as reference; Asian subgroups, Hispanic, non-Hispanic black)
I.description: Self-identified racial/ethnic minority status versus non-Hispanic white

C.description: Non-Hispanic white patients with type 2 diabetes

O.primary_outcome: Prevalence of proteinuric diabetic kidney disease (proteinuria with or without low eGFR)
secondary_outcomes: Prevalence of nonproteinuric DKD (low eGFR alone)
O.measurement: Electronic health record abstraction of proteinuria and eGFR ≤60 mL/min/1.73 m²

variables: traditional DKD risk factors (not fully listed but referenced)

parsed.causal_question: Does racial/ethnic minority status affect the prevalence of proteinuric or nonproteinuric diabetic kidney disease in adults with type 2 diabetes, compared with non-Hispanic whites, after adjustment for traditional risk factors?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: Adults with type 2 diabetes and no prior kidney disease
entry.demographics: Age- and sex-standardized; racial/ethnic groups: non-Hispanic white, Asian (Asian Indian, Chinese, Filipino), Hispanic, non-Hispanic black
entry.setting: Outpatient health care organization, 2008–2010

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)

characteristic.description: Race/ethnicity (non-Hispanic white as reference; Asian subgroups, Hispanic, non-Hispanic black)

entry.description: Self-identified racial/ethnic minority status versus non-Hispanic white

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: Non-Hispanic white patients with type 2 diabetes

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: Prevalence of proteinuric diabetic kidney disease (proteinuria with or without low eGFR)
secondary_outcomes: Prevalence of nonproteinuric DKD (low eGFR alone)
entry.measurement: Electronic health record abstraction of proteinuria and eGFR ≤60 mL/min/1.73 m²

entry.name: Confounders

characteristic.description: traditional DKD risk factors (not fully listed but referenced)

entry.description: Multivariable logistic regression

fhir.causal_question: Does racial/ethnic minority status affect the prevalence of proteinuric or nonproteinuric diabetic kidney disease in adults with type 2 diabetes, compared with non-Hispanic whites, after adjustment for traditional risk factors?

P.description: Veterans undergoing outpatient colonoscopy with split-dose bowel preparation
P.demographics: mean age 60.6 ± 10.5 y; 93.8% male
P.setting: Richard L. Roudebush VA Medical Center, Indianapolis, Indiana (tertiary-care academic VAMC), 2009

I.description: obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²) as the primary exposure

C.description: patients with BMI <30 kg/m²

O.primary_outcome: inadequate bowel preparation quality (Aronchick scale: fair/poor vs excellent/good)
O.measurement: endoscopist-rated Aronchick scale at time of colonoscopy

parsed.causal_question: Does BMI ≥30 kg/m² increase the odds of inadequate split-dose bowel preparation in veterans undergoing outpatient colonoscopy compared to BMI <30 kg/m², after adjusting for comorbidities and medication use?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: Veterans undergoing outpatient colonoscopy with split-dose bowel preparation
entry.demographics: mean age 60.6 ± 10.5 y; 93.8% male
entry.setting: Richard L. Roudebush VA Medical Center, Indianapolis, Indiana (tertiary-care academic VAMC), 2009

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)
entry.description: obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²) as the primary exposure

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: patients with BMI <30 kg/m²

entry.name: Outcome (O)

entry.measurement: endoscopist-rated Aronchick scale at time of colonoscopy

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: multivariable logistic regression

fhir.causal_question: Does BMI ≥30 kg/m² increase the odds of inadequate split-dose bowel preparation in veterans undergoing outpatient colonoscopy compared to BMI <30 kg/m², after adjusting for comorbidities and medication use?

P.description: Adults with painful musculoskeletal metastatic disease undergoing palliative care
P.demographics: Not explicitly reported
P.setting: Single academic interventional radiology service (Emory University Hospital, Atlanta GA and University Hospitals Case Medical Center, Cleveland OH)

I.description: Single-session CT-guided percutaneous cryoablation with two 10–5-min freeze-thaw cycles using 13–17 G cryoprobes

C.description: Pre-procedure baseline (each patient serves as own control)

O.primary_outcome: Change in pain score (Visual Analog Scale)
secondary_outcomes: Change in analgesic requirement (morphine equivalent dosage)
Procedure-related complications within 24 h and at 3 months
O.measurement: VAS pain scores and morphine equivalent dosages recorded before, 24 h, and ~3 months (80 ± 17.7 days) post-procedure

parsed.causal_question: Does CT-guided percutaneous cryoablation reduce pain and analgesic use in patients with painful musculoskeletal metastatic disease compared to baseline, without formal adjustment for confounders?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: Adults with painful musculoskeletal metastatic disease undergoing palliative care
entry.demographics: Not explicitly reported
entry.setting: Single academic interventional radiology service (Emory University Hospital, Atlanta GA and University Hospitals Case Medical Center, Cleveland OH)

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)
entry.description: Single-session CT-guided percutaneous cryoablation with two 10–5-min freeze-thaw cycles using 13–17 G cryoprobes

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: Pre-procedure baseline (each patient serves as own control)

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: Change in pain score (Visual Analog Scale)
secondary_outcomes: Change in analgesic requirement (morphine equivalent dosage)
Procedure-related complications within 24 h and at 3 months
entry.measurement: VAS pain scores and morphine equivalent dosages recorded before, 24 h, and ~3 months (80 ± 17.7 days) post-procedure

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: None reported (descriptive intent-to-treat analysis only)

fhir.causal_question: Does CT-guided percutaneous cryoablation reduce pain and analgesic use in patients with painful musculoskeletal metastatic disease compared to baseline, without formal adjustment for confounders?

P.description: UK general population adults with incident gout vs matched non-gout controls
P.demographics: 18–89 years; sex not specified
P.setting: UK primary-care practices contributing to THIN electronic medical record database

I.description: incident gout diagnosis, split into early (1999–2006) and late (2007–2014) diagnostic cohorts

C.description: matched individuals without gout from the same database and time periods

O.primary_outcome: all-cause mortality
O.measurement: death rates per 1000 person-years and hazard ratios

parsed.causal_question: Does incident gout affect all-cause mortality in UK adults compared to matched non-gout controls after adjusting for demographic and clinical confounders?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: UK general population adults with incident gout vs matched non-gout controls
entry.demographics: 18–89 years; sex not specified
entry.setting: UK primary-care practices contributing to THIN electronic medical record database

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)
entry.description: incident gout diagnosis, split into early (1999–2006) and late (2007–2014) diagnostic cohorts

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: matched individuals without gout from the same database and time periods

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: all-cause mortality
entry.measurement: death rates per 1000 person-years and hazard ratios

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: multivariable Cox regression

fhir.causal_question: Does incident gout affect all-cause mortality in UK adults compared to matched non-gout controls after adjusting for demographic and clinical confounders?

P.description: Adults with acute traumatic spinal cord injury (TSCI) admitted to a regional level I trauma center
P.demographics: Age, sex, race, weight; insurance and education status also recorded
P.setting: Harborview Medical Center (Seattle, WA) model systems database, 2007-2014

I.description: Receipt of methylprednisolone therapy during acute care for TSCI

C.description: Patients who did not receive methylprednisolone during acute care for TSCI

O.primary_outcome: Change in ASIA motor score (0-100) from admission to rehabilitation discharge
O.measurement: ASIA and FIM motor scores extracted from model systems database; infections/GI events from EMR

parsed.causal_question: Does methylprednisolone exposure improve motor function or increase complications in patients with acute traumatic spinal cord injury compared to non-exposure, after adjusting for demographic, injury, and treatment covariates?

entry.description: Patients who did not receive methylprednisolone during acute care for TSCI

entry.description: Multivariable linear regression

fhir.causal_question: Does methylprednisolone exposure improve motor function or increase complications in patients with acute traumatic spinal cord injury compared to non-exposure, after adjusting for demographic, injury, and treatment covariates?

P.description: Members of the Kaiser Permanente Hawaii health system with and without uveitis
P.demographics: mean age 54.1 ± 17.8 years; 127 women, 97 men among 224 uveitis cases
P.setting: Kaiser Permanente Hawaii integrated health system, 2006–2007

I.description: clinical diagnosis of thyroid disease identified via electronic medical record ICD-9 coding

C.description: two control groups selected 4:1 to cases: (1) general Kaiser Permanente Hawaii population, (2) ophthalmology clinic patients without uveitis

O.primary_outcome: diagnosis of uveitis confirmed by fellowship-trained uveitis specialist
O.measurement: electronic medical record query followed by individual chart review

parsed.sample_size: 224 uveitis cases, 896 controls per control group
parsed.causal_question: Does thyroid disease increase the odds of uveitis among Kaiser Permanente Hawaii members compared to those without thyroid disease, after adjusting for age, sex, race, smoking, and autoimmune disease history?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: Members of the Kaiser Permanente Hawaii health system with and without uveitis
entry.demographics: mean age 54.1 ± 17.8 years; 127 women, 97 men among 224 uveitis cases
entry.setting: Kaiser Permanente Hawaii integrated health system, 2006–2007

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)
entry.description: clinical diagnosis of thyroid disease identified via electronic medical record ICD-9 coding

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: two control groups selected 4:1 to cases: (1) general Kaiser Permanente Hawaii population, (2) ophthalmology clinic patients without uveitis

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: diagnosis of uveitis confirmed by fellowship-trained uveitis specialist
entry.measurement: electronic medical record query followed by individual chart review

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: multivariable logistic regression

fhir.sample_size: 224 uveitis cases, 896 controls per control group
fhir.causal_question: Does thyroid disease increase the odds of uveitis among Kaiser Permanente Hawaii members compared to those without thyroid disease, after adjusting for age, sex, race, smoking, and autoimmune disease history?

P.description: Adults undergoing single-photon emission tomography myocardial perfusion imaging (MPI)
P.demographics: Adults >18 years; 568 total (284 regadenoson, 284 dipyridamole); sex/race not reported
P.setting: Single large community teaching hospital (St John Hospital & Medical Center, Detroit, MI)

I.description: Regadenoson as the pharmacologic vasodilator for MPI

C.description: Dipyridamole as the pharmacologic vasodilator for MPI

O.primary_outcome: Composite occurrence of any documented adverse event
secondary_outcomes: Reason for MPI termination (completion vs premature end due to adverse event)
Use of interventional agents (e.g., aminophylline) to treat adverse events
O.measurement: Retrospective review of electronic medical records

parsed.causal_question: Does regadenoson increase the frequency of adverse events compared with dipyridamole in adults undergoing MPI?

entry.setting: Single large community teaching hospital (St John Hospital & Medical Center, Detroit, MI)

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)
entry.description: Regadenoson as the pharmacologic vasodilator for MPI

entry.name: Comparison (C)

entry.description: Composite occurrence of any documented adverse event
secondary_outcomes: Reason for MPI termination (completion vs premature end due to adverse event)
Use of interventional agents (e.g., aminophylline) to treat adverse events
entry.measurement: Retrospective review of electronic medical records

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: none reported

fhir.causal_question: Does regadenoson increase the frequency of adverse events compared with dipyridamole in adults undergoing MPI?

P.description: Adults with symptomatic acute or chronic left iliocaval venous obstruction undergoing iliocaval venous stenting
P.demographics: Median age 46 years (range 15–86), 80% female (301/376)
P.setting: Two European tertiary centres: Galway University Hospital (Ireland) and CHU Nord, Marseille (France)

variables: left common iliac venous stent placement with cranial extension into the IVC
I.description: Endovascular stenting of the left common iliac vein with intentional protrusion of the stent into the inferior vena cava to ensure secure fixation

C.description: Patients without the studied risk factors (i.e., those without acute DVT, pre-operative contralateral IIV thrombosis, pre-existing IVC filter, anticoagulation non-compliance, or malignant compression)
C.type: internal comparison group within the cohort

O.primary_outcome: Development of right (contralateral) iliac deep venous thrombosis after left iliac stent placement
O.measurement: Duplex ultrasound or CT venography performed pre- and post-operatively; Kaplan–Meier analysis for cumulative incidence

variables: pre-operative contralateral internal iliac vein thrombosis
Confounders.adjustment_method: univariate statistical testing (p-values reported); no multivariable model described

parsed.causal_question: Does left iliac venous stent placement increase the risk of contralateral iliac DVT, and which pre-existing factors are associated with this outcome?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: Adults with symptomatic acute or chronic left iliocaval venous obstruction undergoing iliocaval venous stenting
entry.demographics: Median age 46 years (range 15–86), 80% female (301/376)
entry.setting: Two European tertiary centres: Galway University Hospital (Ireland) and CHU Nord, Marseille (France)

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)

characteristic.description: left common iliac venous stent placement with cranial extension into the IVC

entry.description: Endovascular stenting of the left common iliac vein with intentional protrusion of the stent into the inferior vena cava to ensure secure fixation

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: Patients without the studied risk factors (i.e., those without acute DVT, pre-operative contralateral IIV thrombosis, pre-existing IVC filter, anticoagulation non-compliance, or malignant compression)
entry.type: internal comparison group within the cohort

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: Development of right (contralateral) iliac deep venous thrombosis after left iliac stent placement
entry.measurement: Duplex ultrasound or CT venography performed pre- and post-operatively; Kaplan–Meier analysis for cumulative incidence

entry.name: Confounders

characteristic.description: pre-operative contralateral internal iliac vein thrombosis

entry.description: univariate statistical testing (p-values reported); no multivariable model described

fhir.causal_question: Does left iliac venous stent placement increase the risk of contralateral iliac DVT, and which pre-existing factors are associated with this outcome?

P.description: Adults with type 1 or type 2 diabetes without prior foot ulceration
P.demographics: Mean age 69.4 years (range 16–89); 1,370 with type 1 and 12,585 with type 2 diabetes; sex distribution not specified
P.setting: Primary-care cohort, UK

I.description: Development of a new diabetic foot ulcer and level of social deprivation

C.description: No foot ulcer; lowest deprivation quintile
C.type: unexposed group (no ulcer) and least deprived quintile

O.primary_outcome: All-cause mortality
O.measurement: Cox proportional hazards models

parsed.causal_question: Does incident foot ulceration affect all-cause mortality in adults with type 1 or type 2 diabetes, compared to those without ulceration, after adjusting for social deprivation and clinical covariates?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: Adults with type 1 or type 2 diabetes without prior foot ulceration
entry.demographics: Mean age 69.4 years (range 16–89); 1,370 with type 1 and 12,585 with type 2 diabetes; sex distribution not specified
entry.setting: Primary-care cohort, UK

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)
entry.description: Development of a new diabetic foot ulcer and level of social deprivation

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: No foot ulcer; lowest deprivation quintile
entry.type: unexposed group (no ulcer) and least deprived quintile

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: All-cause mortality
entry.measurement: Cox proportional hazards models

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: multivariable Cox regression

fhir.causal_question: Does incident foot ulceration affect all-cause mortality in adults with type 1 or type 2 diabetes, compared to those without ulceration, after adjusting for social deprivation and clinical covariates?

P.description: Adults ≥18 years presenting to 6 EDs for acute ethanol intoxication (primary diagnosis) 2012-2016
P.demographics: Median age 37 y, 69% male
P.setting: Multi-center: 1 county Level-1 trauma, 2 urban university, 1 urban psych/chemical-dependency referral, 2 suburban, 1 rural community EDs in Minnesota

I.description: Clinical management and temporal factors hypothesised to prolong ED stay

C.description: Absence of the exposure variables (no parenteral sedation, no labs ordered, arrival outside 18:00-24:00)
C.type: unexposed/reference group within the same cohort

O.primary_outcome: ED length of stay (LOS) in minutes from arrival to discharge
O.measurement: EMR timestamp data (median 348 min, range 43-1658)

Confounders.adjustment_method: mixed-effects generalized linear model with random intercept for hospital

parsed.causal_question: Do parenteral sedation, laboratory testing, or evening ED arrival increase ED length of stay in adults with acute ethanol intoxication compared to those without these factors, after adjusting for age, sex, ethanol level, psychiatric disposition, frequent intoxication visits, CT use, and daily ED volume?

entry.description: Adults ≥18 years presenting to 6 EDs for acute ethanol intoxication (primary diagnosis) 2012-2016
entry.demographics: Median age 37 y, 69% male
entry.setting: Multi-center: 1 county Level-1 trauma, 2 urban university, 1 urban psych/chemical-dependency referral, 2 suburban, 1 rural community EDs in Minnesota

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)
entry.description: Clinical management and temporal factors hypothesised to prolong ED stay

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: Absence of the exposure variables (no parenteral sedation, no labs ordered, arrival outside 18:00-24:00)
entry.type: unexposed/reference group within the same cohort

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: ED length of stay (LOS) in minutes from arrival to discharge
entry.measurement: EMR timestamp data (median 348 min, range 43-1658)

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: mixed-effects generalized linear model with random intercept for hospital

fhir.causal_question: Do parenteral sedation, laboratory testing, or evening ED arrival increase ED length of stay in adults with acute ethanol intoxication compared to those without these factors, after adjusting for age, sex, ethanol level, psychiatric disposition, frequent intoxication visits, CT use, and daily ED volume?

P.description: Patients with ischemic stroke and confirmed Chagas disease admitted to Brazilian rehabilitation hospitals
P.demographics: Mean age 61 years at stroke onset; sex distribution not specified in abstract
P.setting: SARAH Network of Rehabilitation Hospitals across 8 cities in Brazil (public, open-access, quaternary care)

variables: Chagas disease (confirmed by two serological tests)
I.description: Presence of chronic Chagas disease diagnosed by positive ELISA and hemagglutination assay

C.description: Implicit comparison of patients with versus without specific risk factors within the Chagas cohort; no external control group
C.type: Internal comparison (risk-factor analysis within exposed cohort)

O.primary_outcome: All-cause mortality
O.measurement: Mortality tracked until 2016 via medical records; recurrence ascertained from record review

Confounders.adjustment_method: Multivariable Cox regression for mortality; multivariable logistic regression for recurrence

parsed.causal_question: Among patients with ischemic stroke and Chagas disease, do clinical factors such as age, initial disability, diabetes, and bladder dysfunction affect mortality or stroke recurrence compared to those without these factors, after multivariable adjustment?

characteristic.description: Chagas disease (confirmed by two serological tests)

entry.description: Presence of chronic Chagas disease diagnosed by positive ELISA and hemagglutination assay

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: Implicit comparison of patients with versus without specific risk factors within the Chagas cohort; no external control group
entry.type: Internal comparison (risk-factor analysis within exposed cohort)

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: All-cause mortality
entry.measurement: Mortality tracked until 2016 via medical records; recurrence ascertained from record review

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: Multivariable Cox regression for mortality; multivariable logistic regression for recurrence

fhir.causal_question: Among patients with ischemic stroke and Chagas disease, do clinical factors such as age, initial disability, diabetes, and bladder dysfunction affect mortality or stroke recurrence compared to those without these factors, after multivariable adjustment?

P.description: All patients admitted to a tertiary vascular unit in one year who had an abdominal CT scan within 12 months of admission
P.demographics: Age and sex reported (exact ranges not specified); 314 total patients
P.setting: Tertiary vascular unit, Aberdeen Royal Infirmary, UK

variables: Sarcopaenia defined by skeletal muscle index thresholds
I.description: Sarcopaenia: skeletal muscle index <41 cm²/m² in females and non-obese males, <53 cm²/m² in obese males, measured on CT at L3

C.description: Non-sarcopaenic patients (above the defined skeletal muscle index thresholds)

O.primary_outcome: 3-year mortality
O.measurement: Mortality and discharge destination ascertained at 3 years

parsed.causal_question: Does sarcopaenia affect 3-year mortality and non-home discharge in vascular surgical patients compared to non-sarcopaenic patients, after adjusting for age, sex, and diagnosis type?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: All patients admitted to a tertiary vascular unit in one year who had an abdominal CT scan within 12 months of admission
entry.demographics: Age and sex reported (exact ranges not specified); 314 total patients
entry.setting: Tertiary vascular unit, Aberdeen Royal Infirmary, UK

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)

characteristic.description: Sarcopaenia defined by skeletal muscle index thresholds

entry.description: Sarcopaenia: skeletal muscle index <41 cm²/m² in females and non-obese males, <53 cm²/m² in obese males, measured on CT at L3

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: Non-sarcopaenic patients (above the defined skeletal muscle index thresholds)

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: 3-year mortality
entry.measurement: Mortality and discharge destination ascertained at 3 years

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: Multivariate regression

fhir.causal_question: Does sarcopaenia affect 3-year mortality and non-home discharge in vascular surgical patients compared to non-sarcopaenic patients, after adjusting for age, sex, and diagnosis type?

P.description: Adult patients with type 2 diabetes and uncontrolled glycemia (HbA1c ≥8.0%) on at least one oral antidiabetes agent
P.demographics: Age ≥18 years; sex, race/ethnicity (White vs Hispanic vs other) reported
P.setting: Single large accountable care organization (ACO) in the United States, 2011-2015

variables: timely treatment intensification within 120 days after index HbA1c ≥8.0%
I.description: Addition or switch of antidiabetes medication(s) within a 120-day window following the index elevated HbA1c

C.description: No treatment intensification within the 120-day window (continued prior regimen)

O.primary_outcome: Achievement of HbA1c <8.0% (64 mmol/mol) on any post-index test
secondary_outcomes: Poor control defined as HbA1c >9.0% (75 mmol/mol)
O.measurement: HbA1c values extracted from electronic medical records after the 120-day intensification window

variables: number of unique oral antidiabetes agents before index
Confounders.adjustment_method: two-level hierarchical generalized linear model with patients nested within physicians

parsed.sample_size: 480 patients with ≥1 follow-up HbA1c after the intensification window
parsed.causal_question: Does timely treatment intensification increase the likelihood of achieving HbA1c <8.0% among adults with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes on oral agents, compared with no intensification, after adjusting for baseline HbA1c, BMI, prior therapies, demographics and physician specialty?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: Adult patients with type 2 diabetes and uncontrolled glycemia (HbA1c ≥8.0%) on at least one oral antidiabetes agent
entry.demographics: Age ≥18 years; sex, race/ethnicity (White vs Hispanic vs other) reported
entry.setting: Single large accountable care organization (ACO) in the United States, 2011-2015

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)

characteristic.description: timely treatment intensification within 120 days after index HbA1c ≥8.0%

entry.description: Addition or switch of antidiabetes medication(s) within a 120-day window following the index elevated HbA1c

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: No treatment intensification within the 120-day window (continued prior regimen)

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: Achievement of HbA1c <8.0% (64 mmol/mol) on any post-index test
secondary_outcomes: Poor control defined as HbA1c >9.0% (75 mmol/mol)
entry.measurement: HbA1c values extracted from electronic medical records after the 120-day intensification window

entry.name: Confounders

characteristic.description: number of unique oral antidiabetes agents before index

entry.description: two-level hierarchical generalized linear model with patients nested within physicians

fhir.sample_size: 480 patients with ≥1 follow-up HbA1c after the intensification window
fhir.causal_question: Does timely treatment intensification increase the likelihood of achieving HbA1c <8.0% among adults with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes on oral agents, compared with no intensification, after adjusting for baseline HbA1c, BMI, prior therapies, demographics and physician specialty?

P.description: Kidney stone formers undergoing ureteroscopy for upper-tract stone disease
P.demographics: Age, sex, race, BMI, ASA score; Tennessee residents
P.setting: Vanderbilt University Medical Center, Nashville, Tennessee (statewide prescribing database linkage)

variables: Pre-operative opiate exposure (number of prescriptions, days supplied, unique providers)
I.description: Any opiate prescription filled within 30 days before ureteroscopy

C.description: Patients with no pre-operative opiate prescriptions within 30 days before surgery

O.primary_outcome: Persistent opiate use >60 days after ureteroscopy
secondary_outcomes: Additional opiate prescription filled within 30 days after ureteroscopy
O.measurement: Tennessee Controlled Substance Monitoring Database (CSMD) prescription fill records

parsed.causal_question: Does pre-operative opiate exposure increase the risk of prolonged (>60 days) or additional (within 30 days) post-operative opiate use among kidney stone patients undergoing ureteroscopy, compared to those without pre-operative exposure, after adjusting for demographic and surgical factors?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: Kidney stone formers undergoing ureteroscopy for upper-tract stone disease
entry.demographics: Age, sex, race, BMI, ASA score; Tennessee residents
entry.setting: Vanderbilt University Medical Center, Nashville, Tennessee (statewide prescribing database linkage)

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)

characteristic.description: Pre-operative opiate exposure (number of prescriptions, days supplied, unique providers)

entry.description: Any opiate prescription filled within 30 days before ureteroscopy

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: Patients with no pre-operative opiate prescriptions within 30 days before surgery

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: Persistent opiate use >60 days after ureteroscopy
secondary_outcomes: Additional opiate prescription filled within 30 days after ureteroscopy
entry.measurement: Tennessee Controlled Substance Monitoring Database (CSMD) prescription fill records

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: multivariable logistic regression

fhir.causal_question: Does pre-operative opiate exposure increase the risk of prolonged (>60 days) or additional (within 30 days) post-operative opiate use among kidney stone patients undergoing ureteroscopy, compared to those without pre-operative exposure, after adjusting for demographic and surgical factors?

P.description: Adults with positive serum syphilis serology referred for CSF evaluation of possible neurosyphilis
P.demographics: 95.4% male, 74% HIV co-infected
P.setting: Quaternary centre (The Alfred Hospital, Melbourne, Australia); inpatient or outpatient, Jan 2009–May 2016

I.description: CSF analysis (cell count, protein, non-treponemal and treponemal tests) used to classify neurosyphilis likelihood

C.description: Clinical decision to treat or not treat for neurosyphilis irrespective of CSF result
C.type: empirical clinical decision vs CSF-based recommendation

O.primary_outcome: Concordance between CSF-based neurosyphilis classification and subsequent antibiotic strategy
secondary_outcomes: Proportion treated with neurosyphilis regimen among CSF-confirmed cases
Proportion treated without supportive CSF findings
Proportion not treated despite supportive CSF findings
O.measurement: Retrospective chart review of antibiotic regimen prescribed after LP

Confounders.adjustment_method: Descriptive comparison; no formal multivariable adjustment reported

parsed.causal_question: Does CSF analysis concord with subsequent neurosyphilis antibiotic treatment decisions in patients with positive syphilis serology, compared to empirical clinical decision-making, after accounting for HIV status and clinical presentation?

P.description: Consecutive adults with chronic liver disease (CLD) or cirrhosis attending an Ontario hepatology outpatient clinic
P.demographics: median age 53 years; 53% had cirrhosis
P.setting: single outpatient hepatology clinic, Ontario, Canada, May–Aug 2013

variables: hierarchical algorithm combining laboratory values and administrative diagnostic/billing codes
I.description: A multi-step algorithm designed to assign CLD/cirrhosis etiology from routinely collected healthcare data

C.description: Gold-standard etiology assigned by an attending hepatologist using full chart abstraction (clinical, lab, imaging, histology)

O.primary_outcome: Diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, κ agreement) of the algorithm for each etiology
O.measurement: Sensitivity, specificity, positive and negative predictive values, Cohen’s kappa versus hepatologist assignment

Confounders.adjustment_method: None (validation study comparing algorithm to reference; no causal effect estimated)

parsed.causal_question: Does the hierarchical algorithm accurately reproduce hepatologist-assigned CLD/cirrhosis etiology in administrative data?

P.description: All patients undergoing below-the-knee amputation (BKA) by the orthopaedic surgery service at a single Level 1 trauma center
P.demographics: Age, sex, race/ethnicity not fully reported; 28% trauma, 57% infection, 15% malignancy indications
P.setting: Single Level 1 trauma center, Oregon Health & Science University, 2008–2018

I.description: Index BKA procedure, with some staged closures

C.description: Patients with hemoglobin A1c ≤ 8.1% (reference group for primary analysis); no explicit unexposed group
C.type: internal comparison by glycemic control level

O.primary_outcome: Unplanned reoperation (irrigation & débridement, stump revision, or revision to higher-level amputation) within 1 year of index BKA
secondary_outcomes: 30-day limb survivorship free from unplanned reoperation
O.measurement: Kaplan-Meier estimation and multivariate logistic regression

parsed.causal_question: Does poor glycemic control (HbA1c > 8.1%) increase the odds of unplanned reoperation within 1 year after BKA compared to adequate glycemic control (HbA1c ≤ 8.1%), after adjusting for age, gender, platelet count, albumin, and amputation indication?

P.description: Adults undergoing head and neck oncologic surgery with free-flap reconstruction at a single cancer center
P.demographics: age, sex, race not detailed in excerpt
P.setting: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston TX

variables: ERAS pathway (HNRS-ERAS) including patient education, goal-directed fluid therapy, multimodal analgesia, early mobilization

risk factors for high opioid usage
O.measurement: NSQIP database for complications; electronic medical records for pain scores and opioid consumption

parsed.causal_question: Does implementation of an ERAS pathway reduce perioperative complications, ICU admissions, hospital LOS, and opioid usage in patients undergoing head and neck oncologic surgery with free-flap reconstruction compared with usual care, after adjusting for relevant confounders?

P.description: Adult and pediatric patients visiting the emergency department of a large tertiary academic urban hospital
P.demographics: Mean age 49.2 years (SD 19.1); 59.4% female; 76.7% non-Hispanic
P.setting: Emergency department, Brigham and Women’s Hospital, Boston, Massachusetts, USA

variables: patient photograph displayed in EHR banner
I.description: Non-interruptive display of the patient’s photograph in the electronic health record banner during order entry

C.description: Orders placed for patients whose photograph was NOT displayed in the EHR banner

O.primary_outcome: Wrong-patient order entry (WPOE) error rate measured by the retract-and-reorder method
secondary_outcomes: WPOE error rate in any care setting (not restricted to ED)
O.measurement: Retract-and-reorder alert: an order is retracted within 10 min and reordered for a different patient within the next 10 min

variables: hour of day, day of week, month-year
number of open encounters per clinician

parsed.causal_question: Does displaying patient photographs in the EHR banner reduce the rate of wrong-patient order entry errors in ED patients compared with orders placed without a photograph displayed, after adjusting for patient acuity, race, temporal factors, clinician characteristics, and workload?

P.description: Adults with hypertension and/or diabetes enrolled in the Taizhou electronic health-record system
P.demographics: Age ≥18 y (65+ highlighted), sex not specified, rural (agricultural hukou) vs urban residence

variables: primary durable LVAD implantation as destination therapy

C.description: none (single-arm cohort; outcomes compared across implant eras or against known benchmarks)

O.primary_outcome: 1-year mortality (all-cause)

parsed.causal_question: Does destination-therapy LVAD implantation affect 1-year mortality in non-transplant end-stage heart failure patients, after adjusting for renal function, CPB time, hemoglobin and acuity?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: Adults ≥18 years with end-stage heart failure undergoing primary LVAD implantation as destination therapy at a single non-transplant center
entry.demographics: mean age 65.7 years, 80% male, 51% ischemic etiology, 65% INTERMACS 1–2
entry.setting: Baylor Scott & White The Heart Hospital, Plano, Texas (non-transplant DT-LVAD program), 2010–2021

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)

characteristic.description: primary durable LVAD implantation as destination therapy

entry.description: implantation of a durable left-ventricular assist device for destination therapy

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: none (single-arm cohort; outcomes compared across implant eras or against known benchmarks)

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: 1-year mortality (all-cause)
entry.measurement: Kaplan–Meier survival curves; multivariable Cox regression for 1-year mortality

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression

fhir.causal_question: Does destination-therapy LVAD implantation affect 1-year mortality in non-transplant end-stage heart failure patients, after adjusting for renal function, CPB time, hemoglobin and acuity?
